body: Typo in comment: “compatibalize” should be corrected (e.g., “compatibilize” / “provide backwards compatibility”) to avoid propagating misspellings in inline documentation.

This example’s assertion is too weak to validate the intended behavior. `include('subsidiaries')` would still pass even if the leading copyright line wasn’t stripped (since the original content also contains “Subsidiaries”). Consider asserting both that the continuation remains and that the copyright notice line is removed (e.g., expectation that normalized content does not contain “copyright”/the first-line text).
```suggestion
        expect(normalized_content).to include('subsidiaries')
        expect(normalized_content).not_to include('copyright (c) 2020 by corporation name and')
```

The paragraph-stripping guard doesn’t currently require a copyright notice to be present. Because `copyright_regex` is a union that includes `REGEXES[:all_rights_reserved]`, `p =~ copyright_regex` will be true for paragraphs that only contain “All rights reserved”, so the opening paragraph could be stripped even when no copyright notice exists. To match the PR’s stated intent, check for `Matchers::Copyright::REGEX` (or another explicit copyright-notice regex) separately from the “all rights reserved” marker, rather than using the union for both checks.
```suggestion
        copyright_notice_regex = Matchers::Copyright::REGEX
        all_rights_reserved_regex = ContentHelper::REGEXES[:all_rights_reserved]
        copyright_regex = Regexp.union(copyright_notice_regex, all_rights_reserved_regex)
        # Strip opening paragraph only when both a copyright notice and
        # "All rights reserved." are present — confirms attribution, not license text.
        strip(/\A.*?(?=\n\n)/m) if (p = _content[/\A.*?(?=\n\n)/m]) &&
                                   p =~ copyright_notice_regex &&
                                   all_rights_reserved_regex.match?(p)
```

Comment uses British spelling "normalised" but the rest of this file/codebase consistently uses "normalized"/"normalization". For consistency, consider changing the comment text to "normalized".
```suggestion
        # (e.g. "copyright\nowner") is still normalized correctly.
```

I'd like in addition to have a version of this test without the `All rights reserved` line.

In that case per my feedback on https://github.com/licensee/licensee/issues/951#issuecomment-4148640350 the `its Subsidiaries (see AUTHORS).` line should not be stripped as there's no way to discern that it isn't the beginning of license text.

Memoizing `words` means every caller of `wordset` will now retain the full scanned word sequence (including duplicates) for the lifetime of the object. In the matching pipeline, `Matchers::Dice#potential_matches` calls `license.wordset` for many licenses that may never need `bigrams`, so this can materially increase memory usage vs. scanning transiently.

Consider either (a) only caching `@words` when `bigrams` is actually requested, or (b) deduplicating the scanned strings when caching (e.g., converting each scanned token to an fstring) so repeated words don’t keep many duplicate String objects alive.

In the docstring for `bigram_similarity`, the sentence “Unlike wordset similarity this is sensitive …” is missing a comma after “similarity”, which makes it read awkwardly. Please adjust the punctuation (and optionally reflow the sentence) for clarity.
```suggestion
      # over bigrams (consecutive word pairs).  Unlike wordset similarity, this
```

PR description says the rate-limit guidance is added as a subsection under “Using Licensee with Docker”, but the new “Providing an access token” section is placed before the Docker section and the Docker portion doesn’t explicitly call out rate limits. Either adjust the doc structure/heading to match the intended Docker-focused guidance or update the PR description to reflect the current placement.

In the Docker example, `docker run -e OCTOKIT_ACCESS_TOKEN ...` relies on the variable already being set in the host environment (it passes through by name). Consider using an explicit assignment form (or referencing the host value) to make the copy/paste behavior and intent clearer for readers.
```suggestion
docker run -e OCTOKIT_ACCESS_TOKEN="$OCTOKIT_ACCESS_TOKEN" licensee detect rails/rails --remote
```

The access token example uses `export OCTOKIT_ACCESS_TOKEN=...`, which leaves the token in the shell environment for subsequent commands. Elsewhere in the docs the pattern is to set the variable for a single command invocation (see `docs/usage.md` “Providing an access token”). Consider switching this snippet to the one-command form for consistency and to avoid persisting the token longer than needed.

PR description says the token docs are added as a subsection under the Docker section, but this change also introduces a standalone "Providing an access token" section before the Docker heading. If the intent is Docker-only guidance, consider moving this content under "Using Licensee with Docker"; otherwise, update the PR description to match the broader CLI docs change.

The examples embed the token literal in the command (`OCTOKIT_ACCESS_TOKEN=your_token_here` / `docker run -e OCTOKIT_ACCESS_TOKEN=your_token_here`), which is easy to copy/paste but can leak secrets into shell history/process listings. Consider showing a safer pattern (e.g., exporting the env var first, using `docker run -e OCTOKIT_ACCESS_TOKEN` to forward from the host environment, or using an `--env-file`).

There are two consecutive blank lines between the access-token example and the next section header. Consider reducing this to a single blank line to keep Markdown spacing consistent.
```suggestion

```

Line has trailing whitespace in the header (`### Providing an access token `). This creates noisy diffs and can trip whitespace-sensitive linters/editors; please remove the trailing space.
```suggestion
### Providing an access token
```

Done in f04714e. The wording now says "SPDX-style identifier format" with concrete examples and explicitly notes that Licensee checks the format only and does not validate against the official SPDX license list. Also replaced "text-ish" with "commonly used text extension".
@copilot apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/licensee/licensee/pull/973#discussion_r3034784906)

The phrase “standard text-ish extension” is a bit informal for user-facing documentation. Consider using a more precise term like “text-based extension” or “commonly used text extensions” for clarity.

This text says the LICENSES/ filename must match a *valid* SPDX license identifier, but the implementation only enforces an SPDX-style pattern (letters/numbers with dashes/dots) and does not validate against the official SPDX license list. Consider rewording to avoid implying strict SPDX-list validation (e.g., “SPDX identifier format / SPDX-style identifier”, and optionally link to the SPDX identifier syntax).

Removing `baz` from `HashHelperSpecFixture` makes the later example `it 'does not expose other methods'` (which asserts `hash` has no `:baz` key) effectively vacuous, since the fixture no longer defines any non-`HASH_METHODS` instance method to potentially leak into `to_h`. To keep this spec meaningfully validating that `HashHelper#to_h` only includes methods listed in `HASH_METHODS`, add a non-listed method back (could be renamed) or update the spec to assert the intended behavior in another way.
```suggestion
  end

  # Method not listed in HASH_METHODS; used to ensure HashHelper#to_h
  # does not expose arbitrary instance methods.
  def baz
    'not included'
  end
```
Seems this was added in f5c4c7ff4ee43ea190bd7171b48695da44c1403b and never used.

The PR title/description says the fix is to change the gemspec’s version loading (replace `require` with `File.read` + regex), but this PR only changes the release workflow by splitting `gem bump --release` into separate `gem bump` and `gem release` commands. Please either update the PR metadata to match the actual change, or include the gemspec change if that’s the intended fix (current `licensee.gemspec` still `require`s `lib/licensee/version`).
Can't be certain such a line isn't the beginning of license text, so sticking with this limited fix for now.

`CONTINUATION_LINE_REGEX` currently only matches indented continuation lines that begin with a 4-digit year (`\d{4}`), but the PR description/intent is to strip any indented continuation line following a copyright line (e.g., lines that begin with a name/company and omit the year). This narrower pattern may still miss real-world multi-holder blocks where subsequent lines don’t start with a year. Consider changing the continuation pattern to require indentation plus a non-whitespace char (e.g. `
[ 	]+\S[^
]*`) and adding a spec case that covers a non-year continuation line.
```suggestion
      CONTINUATION_LINE_REGEX = /
[ 	]+\S[^
]*/
```

The new `html_comment` fixture only exercises a single-line HTML comment, so it doesn't actually verify the `/m` (dot matches newlines) behavior that the regex change is intended to add. Consider making the fixture include a newline inside the comment (and/or adding a second fixture) so the spec will fail if multiline comments aren't stripped correctly.

The wording "for Ruby projects" reads like generic guidance, but the commands that follow (e.g., `script/bootstrap`) are repository-specific. Consider rephrasing to clearly scope this to this repository (e.g., "For this repo (Ruby)...") or otherwise indicate these are example commands, to avoid confusion if this workflow guidance is reused elsewhere.

Line 153 is indented one space less than the other sub-steps (a–c/e–g). In Markdown this can break the nested list formatting and make step (d) render incorrectly. Align the indentation with the surrounding items (same leading spaces as lines 150–152 and 154–156).
@copilot open a new pull request to apply changes based on [this feedback](https://github.com/licensee/licensee/pull/944#discussion_r2898362760)

`match` calls `license_property` multiple times (including inside `find` and again in `match_by_spdx_base_key`), so subclasses may re-run their regex parsing several times per `match` call. Consider storing `license_property` in a local variable (and reusing the same `licenses` collection) and passing the value into the helper so parsing happens once.

The error message could be more readable with improved punctuation. Consider changing line 63 to add a period after the URL:

"Unsupported remote URL: #{path}. " \

This would make the message read more naturally as two separate sentences.
```suggestion
      "Unsupported remote URL: #{path}. " \
```

The test description "Returns a one exit code" is grammatically awkward. Consider using "Returns exit code 1" or "Returns an exit code of 1" instead. Note: This pattern exists elsewhere in the file (line 140), so this comment applies to maintaining consistency - if changed here, consider updating the other instance as well.

The new empty-directory coverage exercises the default (YAML-ish) output, but the reported bug (#576) is for `licensee detect --json <empty dir>` returning exit status 1. Add a spec case for `--json` with an empty directory asserting exit status 0 and the expected JSON structure (e.g., empty `licenses`/`matched_files`) so the regression is covered.

The comment documenting the file hash keys has a typo: "the the following keys" should be "the following keys" (duplicate "the").
```suggestion
      # Hashes will have the following keys:
```

The comment documenting the file hash keys has a typo: "the the following keys" should be "the following keys" (duplicate "the").
```suggestion
      # Hashes will have the following keys:
```

A blank line was removed between lines 143 and 144. While this is a minor formatting change, it appears to be an unrelated whitespace cleanup that doesn't contribute to the PR's main objective of supporting LICENSES/ directories. Consider whether this formatting change is necessary for this PR.
```suggestion
    handle_json_output if options[:json]
```

The test description says "returns both licenses" but the expectation is for only 1 license. This should be "returns the license" to accurately reflect what is being tested.
```suggestion
    it 'returns the license', :aggregate_failures do
```

The use of `and` operator instead of `&&` can be problematic due to its lower precedence. In this context, the expression `e[:type] == :tree and e[:name] == 'LICENSES'` works correctly, but Ruby style guides generally recommend using `&&` for boolean operations to avoid precedence issues. Consider changing to `e[:type] == :tree && e[:name] == 'LICENSES'`.
```suggestion
        licenses_dir = commit.tree.find { |e| e[:type] == :tree && e[:name] == 'LICENSES' }
```

Several fixtures were added to fixtures.yml but lack corresponding test coverage:
- licenses-dir-with-license-ref: Should verify that LicenseRef-* filenames are accepted in LICENSES/ directories
- licenses-dir-with-multiple-license-files: Should verify behavior when LICENSES/ contains multiple license files
- licenses-dir-with-top-level-license: Should verify precedence between top-level LICENSE and LICENSES/ directory files

These fixtures should have tests in spec/licensee/project_spec.rb similar to how 'licenses-dir' is tested for GitProject (lines 78-101), or in dedicated test contexts for FSProject and GitHubProject.

The LICENSES_FILENAME_REGEX lacks comprehensive test coverage for edge cases. While basic tests exist for valid SPDX names and invalid names with spaces, there should be additional tests for:
- LicenseRef-* patterns (e.g., "LicenseRef-MIT.txt", "LicenseRef-Custom-1.0.md")
- Edge cases like filenames starting with a digit (e.g., "0BSD.txt")
- Filenames with dots in the SPDX identifier (e.g., "GPL-2.0.txt")
- Invalid patterns like filenames starting with a dash or dot before the extension

The signature of `LicenseFile.name_score` has changed from `name_score(filename)` to `name_score(dir, filename)`. This is a breaking API change that could affect external callers. While the internal usage has been updated throughout the codebase, any external code calling this method directly will break. Consider documenting this breaking change in the PR description or release notes, or providing a deprecation path by making the dir parameter optional with a default value.

The LICENSES_FILENAME_REGEX allows filenames like "LicenseRef-.txt" or "LicenseRef-..txt" because the pattern `LicenseRef-[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` permits any combination of letters, numbers, dots, and dashes after "LicenseRef-", including starting immediately with a dot or dash. According to the REUSE specification, a LicenseRef should have a meaningful identifier. Consider using `LicenseRef-[A-Za-z0-9][-A-Za-z0-9.]*` to ensure the identifier starts with an alphanumeric character, or at minimum `LicenseRef-[A-Za-z0-9.-]*[A-Za-z0-9]` to prevent starting/ending with special characters.
```suggestion
      LICENSES_FILENAME_REGEX = /\A(?:LicenseRef-[A-Za-z0-9](?:[-A-Za-z0-9.]*[A-Za-z0-9])?|[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9.-]*)#{PREFERRED_EXT_REGEX}\z/i
```

Changes to these dependency files would affect the Docker build and should trigger the workflow. Consider adding these paths to the filter list.
```suggestion

      - Gemfile
      - licensee.gemspec
      - .github/workflows/docker.yml

    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - Dockerfile
      - Gemfile
      - licensee.gemspec
```

Changing from `sub('*', 'u')` to `tr('*', 'u')` changes the behavior. The `sub` method replaces the first occurrence of the pattern with the replacement string, while `tr` translates characters. For example, if `license.name` is "MIT*", `sub('*', 'u')` would produce "MITu", while `tr('*', 'u')` would also produce "MITu". However, if the name contains multiple asterisks, `tr` would replace all of them, not just the first one. The original code used `sub`, which should be preserved to maintain the same behavior.

          license_variation = license.title.sub('*', 'u')
```

```

Changing from `sub('*', 'u')` to `tr('*', 'u')` alters the behavior. The `sub` method replaces only the first occurrence of '*' with 'u', while `tr` translates all occurrences of '*' to 'u'. If a license name contains multiple asterisks, this change will produce different results than the original code. The original `sub` method should be preserved.

The word "doesn'tincludes" should be "doesn't include" (with a space between "doesn't" and "include").
Same issue as above - `tr('*', 'u')` should be `sub('*', 'u')` to match the original behavior.

```

```

I might be missing something but a chatbot seems to agree 🙃 

> Certainly! Let's dive into the world of Git and GitHub paths.
> 
> 1. **Git Reference (Git Ref)**:
>     - A **Git reference** (often called a **git ref**) is a file that contains a **Git commit SHA-1 hash**.
>     - When referring to a specific Git commit, you can use the **Git reference** as an easy-to-remember name instead of dealing with the raw hash.
>     - Git references can be rewritten to point to new commits. For example:
>         - A **branch** is a Git reference that stores the latest Git commit hash for that branch.
>         - A **tag** is another type of Git reference that points to a specific commit (usually used for releases).
>     - Instead of saying "commit `a3b7f9c2e`," you can say "the `main` branch" or "the `v1.0` tag."
> 
> 2. **GitHub PATH**:
>     - The term "GitHub PATH" might be a bit confusing because it's not a standard Git concept.
>     - However, I suspect you might be referring to **GitHub REST API endpoints** related to Git references.
>     - These endpoints allow you to interact with references (branches, tags, etc.) in your Git database on GitHub.
>     - For example, you can use the GitHub API to:
>         - List matching references based on a name (e.g., branches or tags).
>         - Retrieve information about a specific reference (e.g., commit SHA-1 hash).
>         - Create or update references.
>     - The **GitHub PATH** here refers to the URL path you use to access these API endpoints.
> 
> 3. **Example API Endpoint**:
>     - To list matching references (branches or tags) in a GitHub repository, you can use the following API endpoint:
>         - `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/matching-refs/{ref}`
>     - Here:
>         - `{owner}` is the account owner (case-insensitive).
>         - `{repo}` is the repository name (without the `.git` extension).
>         - `{ref}` can be a commit SHA, branch name (e.g., `heads/main`), or tag name (e.g., `tags/v1.0`).
>     - The response will include information about the matching references.
> 
> 4. **Example Usage**:
>     - Suppose you want to list matching references for the `main` branch in a repository owned by `octocat`. You'd make a request like this:
>         - `GET /repos/octocat/my-repo/git/matching-refs/heads/main`
> 
> Remember, Git references and GitHub API endpoints play a crucial role in managing version control and collaboration within repositories! [🌟](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/git/refs)¹²³..
> 
> Source: Conversation with Bing, 3/[1](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/git/refs)[2](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Internals-The-Refspec)/2024
> (1) REST API endpoints for Git references - GitHub Docs. https://docs.github.com/en/rest/git/refs.
> (2) Git - The Refspec. https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Internals-The-Refspec.
> ([3](https://superuser.com/questions/614978/how-to-reference-a-git-repository)) path - How to reference a git repository? - Super User. https://superuser.com/questions/614978/how-to-reference-a-git-repository.
> (4) Git - git-show-ref Documentation. https://git-scm.com/docs/git-show-ref.
> (5) undefined. https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO/git/matching-refs/REF.
> (6) undefined. https://github.com/schacon/simplegit-progit.

I found a better implementation that didn't require the comment. In short, Ruby's `Dir.glob` command ignores Unix-style hidden files (e.g., `.licensee.yml`), which you need to instruct it to include, via the `File::FNM_DOTMATCH` flag).

I agree with your assessment that licenses.nuget.org isn't especially useful - most of the time it's used, there's a `<license>` expression anyways since those features were added to nuget around the same time.

But there's arguably a question of ownership between `licensee/licensee` and `github/licensed` (where I'm contributing https://github.com/github/licensed/pull/261) when it comes to `licenseUrl`. My understanding is that fetching and matching the content of the URL (when it isn't well-known) might not meet the goals of `licensee` - I don't think any other project type in `licensee` currently downloads license content from the internet, whereas `licensed` already does that for some package types.

It seemed difficult to split it between the two, since `licensed` would presumably want to know if the match for `package_file` was due to a `license` or `licenseUrl`, so it could avoid downloading the license if it was well-known, but still download it in the case where `licensee` found a `license` but `licenseUrl` was a non-well-known URL and potentially a different license. package.rb doesn't currently have a mechanism to expose something like that.

After some digging, I can clarify the difference. This may be more detail than you wanted.
- [`GitProject#load_file`](url) uses `Rugged::Blob.to_buffer` which calls down to a [C Binding](https://github.com/libgit2/rugged/blob/master/ext/rugged/rugged_blob.c#L496). 
- The [`rb_str_new`](https://github.com/libgit2/rugged/blob/master/ext/rugged/rugged_blob.c#L529) call in that method is a Ruby C method which converts a `char*` to a Ruby String with a default encoding of `ASCII-8BIT`. https://silverhammermba.github.io/emberb/c/#string. (Can't find official docs.)

The `FSProject#load_file` is using `File.read` which automatically assumes a default encoding of `UTF-8`.

So the same byte that's an invalid `UTF-8` character isn't an invalid `ASCII-8BIT` character and the `GitProject` tests actually don't fail.

```ruby
# UTF-8
f = File.read('spec/fixtures/readme-invalid-encoding/README.md')
=> "# Readme fixture\n\n ... \n\^P\xFF\xFF"
f.encoding
=> #<Encoding:UTF-8>
f.valid_encoding?
=> false

# ASCII-8BIT
# Use invocation that specifies a length and forces ASCII-8BIT, emulating rb_str_new behavior
# https://ruby-doc.org/core-2.5.0/IO.html#method-i-read
f_ascii = File.read('spec/fixtures/readme-invalid-encoding/README.md', 1087)
=> "# Readme fixture\n\n ... \n\^P\xFF\xFF"
f_ascii.encoding
=> #<Encoding:ASCII-8BIT>
f_ascii.valid_encoding?
=> true
```

I don't know of anyone who disagrees supporting the XML format.  This could be accelerated there is a strong demand.  Suggest this be raised with the SPDX legal team and/or SPDX tech team if you would like to accelerate the standardization of the XML format.

It's really not. Titles are used by the reference matcher (for README files) and `CREATIVE COMMONS LEGAL CODE` doesn't make any sense there. It's some cruft added to the license text by the license steward, for better or worse, that is optional/often isn't deployed in the wild, but often is.
Can we dynamically pull this from the license text, e.g., via regex rather than hard coding it twice in the project?

If this is an alternate title, I believe if you add a reference in https://github.com/benbalter/licensee/blob/1553efdd953953818272a60b3ac2185c8aa0e4c4/lib/licensee/license.rb#L88-L92, it should be stripped by existing normalizers.

For context, here's the existing cc0 title regex:

```
/(?i-mx:creative\ commons\ zero\,?\ (?:version\ |v)?1\.0\ universal)|cc0\-1\.0/
```

> I'd love to see guidance from SPDX that the XML is what third parties should consume if they want to leverage SPDX's careful semantic markup.

SPDX is currently warning folks away from the XML toward the old-style templates, e.g. [here][1].

> Is there any hint that is even a goal?

*I* would like the XML to be the preferred interface, since it avoids the under-specification I mention above.  The XML has its own under-specification issues (spdx/license-list-XML#495), but they're at the “what does this mean” level.  That's better than the old-style template's “there's no way to write optional text that contains semcolons” issues.

I'm not aware of folks with SPDX commit access who share my view.  My impression is that the XML will eventually become the recommended format, but it will take a bit for some schema changes (e.g. spdx/license-list-XML#452) to settle.  At the current pace, I'd estimate a year before the XML format stabilizes and then maybe another six months before folks are willing to commit a formal recommendation to consume the XML.

I don't expect the schema changes to be particularly troublesome to keep up with for consumers that pin their license-list-XML dependencies to released versions, so if it was me, I'd make the switch now.  But sticking with the old format and assuming the escaping issues don't come up will *probably* work.  And it will save you a bit of dev time while the XML format settles down.

[1]: https://github.com/spdx/license-list-XML/tree/48a9dda95d6baccaa4eb646557cdc4a7cb45e748#this-repository-is-unstable

@wking I tend to agree; the templates make me want to run away screaming, but I'm not entirely sure that's a rational response. :smile: I'd love to see guidance from SPDX that the XML is what third parties should consume if they want to leverage SPDX's careful semantic markup. Is there any hint that is even a goal?

> You may also want to allow internal parens…

I've filed support for this in #261, although Licensee will still return `other` for those cases.

You may also want to allow internal parens.  They are not yet supported by crates.io (withoutboats/license-exprs#3, and I'm documenting that limitation in rust-lang/cargo#4898), but hopefully they'll be supported at some point.  And I don't see a problem with allowing them in this regex now.

You seem to already have these stubbed out, but these forms are [very weakly specified][1].  For example, the spec does not cover how to escape a semicolon occurring in the original text.  For a much saner upstream format, you can use license-list-XML (e.g. [`ISC.xml`][2]), which leans on XML for all the hard parts.  The downside is that the XML format is [currently unstable][3], but if you vendor the XML for the licenses you need that shouldn't be a problem.

If you stick with your regexp approach and the license-list-data content, I'd recommend using `[^;]*` in your regexps instead of `.+?`.  Although I don't really understand `.+?`, so maybe it's fine.

[1]: https://spdx.org/spdx-specification-21-web-version#h.2mjng0vqrghe
[2]: https://github.com/spdx/license-list-XML/blob/cdb5b301a99c35d2aa83daa47c6ac4eba3a810df/src/ISC.xml
[3]: https://github.com/spdx/license-list-XML/tree/cdb5b301a99c35d2aa83daa47c6ac4eba3a810df#this-repository-is-unstable

You may want to label this `CC0_DISCLAIMER` or some such.  The other CC licenses have similar disclaimers, but the CC0 has additional wording for “information”.  For example, compare the [CC-BY-3.0][1] disclaimer.  The [CC-BY-4.0][2] disclaimer is even more different, dropping caps, adding `("Creative Commons")`, etc.

[1]: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/legalcode.txt
[2]: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode.txt

Adding a comment here (and a few others), but in short we're finding the most similar license to the one we're looking at (we're going to see if it is in the set of similar licenses, not cut off from comparison due to max delta), but not if confidence is set to less than 50 in which case all bets are off, and who would want to set confidence so low anyway (in fact only one pair fails without this).

Yes, GPL and AGPL are long licenses, so they are getting hit hard by dissimilarity scaling with size mentioned at https://github.com/benbalter/licensee/pull/242#discussion_r153684000

Power operator. Base similarity to power of (log10 of wordset size). That's how dissimilarity is  scaled in this proposal.

I'm going to change total to wordset.size as the argument to log10 though, since there's no need for the scaling to be sensitive to the size of the candidate text, and it makes the scaling slightly less aggressive.
To make it easier to monkeypatch to get the uncached version in a test that varies confidence level (which max_delta needs to respond to). I'll just remove the cache; this is not a heavy calculation.

For backwards compatibility, we'll want a class method 

```ruby
def license_dir
  license_dirs.first
end
```

That way, it remains accurate even if overwritten.

> `:replace:` will remove all the invalid characters altogether from the License

Yes, that's exactly what was causing #79.

Trying force_encoding first, and only running encode (which does above) if that doesn't produce a valid encoding should avoid the latter in many cases. (I didn't realize you could check for a valid encoding, that was the hint I needed.)

This is pretty good. Do note that encoding an UTF8-tagged script into UTF-8 with `:replace` will remove all the invalid characters altogether from the License -- as opposed to Charlock's approach that actually figures out the encoding of those characters and transcodes them into valid UTF8...

So we're gonna be losing data here, and hence reducing accuracy. But I think this is an acceptable tradeoff, particularly since the text of all our supported licenses is usually ASCII English and this approach will only strip away things like author names.
That was the hint I needed to avoid Charlock entirely. Thanks! Alternative in #200

I think we need to do better here. Charlock is not free (particularly not the transcoding step). The logic needs to be more like:

```ruby
@content.force_encoding(Encoding::UTF_8)
if !@content.valid_encoding?
  # do the transcoding
end
```

Clarified via 3579d6f. I was wrong. In Licensee, the existence of a LicenseFile shows that there is a license. If no matcher returns a match (e.g., DiceMatcher returns nil for match), that means the license hasn't been detected (but that there is a license file). In the GitHub API, we go a step further and collapse that into `other`, nil, or the detected license.

To make it slightly more future proof, it sounds like the map could be simplified to:

1. Swap the first `_` for a `-`
2. Swap the second `_` for a `.`
3. Replace `Mozilla` with `MPL`
4. Add a `.0` to any GPL'd license

Something like

```ruby
perl_name.sub('_', '-').sub('_', '.').sub('Mozilla', 'MPL').sub(/^GPL-(\d)$/, 'GPL-\1.0')
```

Should theoretically alleviate the need to maintain a map.

Per CRAN policy MIT is what they call a 'license template' (https://www.r-project.org/Licenses/MIT), which states a structured format for `LICENSE` you need to follow to comply and be accepted into CRAN. Deviations from this form will be rejected. So while in principle what you say is accurate, in practice these packages are using the basic MIT license.

You did and we talked about it, but I believe we reached a conclusion that it doesn't matter how regexes are read, because files are tested one by one in sequence. "COPYING" will match the regex and get assigned a score of "1", then LICENSE will match and also get assigned a score of "1", and so on.
This is the question I asked in chat... if regex was read left to right. If so, then I believe LICENSE should win over COPYING which should win over COPYRIGHT.

The backwards-incompatible consequence of this change is that the license filenames are not picked by the order of preference anymore. Now, the "COPYING" file will win over a "LICENSE" file in the same repo, when previously the opposite was the case.
Should the match score be higher if the filename matches a file closer to the beginning of LICENSE_FILENAMES array? It seems that the names in that array are sorted by preference.
